Party_Details.Name: Tan Hup Thye

Facts.Text: From the time of the defendant's incorporation in February 1984, Tan Hup Thye was employed by Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
At the start of his employment in 1984, Tan Hup Thye held the position of executive vice president at Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
In November 1986, Tan Hup Thye became managing director of Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
On 4 December 2005, Tan Hup Thye ceased to hold the position of managing director of Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
After 4 December 2005 and until the commencement of liquidation proceedings, Tan Hup Thye remained a director of Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
On 5 December 2005, Tan Hup Thye accepted an offer of employment with Man Financial (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
On 5 December 2005, the employment of Tan Hup Thye with Man Financial (Singapore) Pte Ltd was terminated pursuant to a termination agreement that provided a termination package to him.
For the period 1 March 2005 to 30 September 2005, a bonus amount of $1,412,759.00 was later specified in internal documents as the bonus figure associated with Tan Hup Thye.
For the months of October and November 2005, a further bonus amount of $47,683.03 was later specified in internal documents as associated with Tan Hup Thye.

On 23 January 1996, a memorandum issued by Tan Hup Thye to all employees of Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd announced that bonus distribution would be on a financial year basis, with an advance bonus payment in January and a final bonus payment around end May after final results, and that a fixed percentage of pre-tax profits would be distributed as bonuses to staff, with individual quantum dependent on departmental profits and individual performance.

On 23 January 1996, Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd communicated to employees that a profit sharing formula based on a fixed percentage of pre-tax profits would be used to determine the total bonus pool for distribution to staff.

On 2 November 2001, in an e-mail to the chief financial officer of Refco Inc, Tan Hup Thye stated that year-end bonuses for Refco Singapore and Refco Investments Pte Ltd were based on actual audited results and payable around May each year, that the provision for bonuses was based on 30% of pre-tax profit adjusted for a 5% return on shareholders' funds, that head office did not determine any bonus in Singapore but that the bonus list was normally approved by Philip Roger Bennett before payment, and that his own bonus came out of this bonus provision and was included in the list approved by Bennett.
For calendar year 1997, Tan Hup Thye received salary of $320,000, bonus of $97,495, and benefits in kind of $25,278, giving total income of $442,773, with bonus forming 22.1% of total income.
For calendar year 1998, Tan Hup Thye received salary of $324,000, bonus of $455,000, and benefits in kind of $75,531, giving total income of $612,163, with bonus forming 53.2% of total income.
For calendar year 1999, Tan Hup Thye received salary of $324,000, bonus of $308,397, and benefits in kind of $31,645, giving total income of $664,042, with bonus forming 46.4% of total income.
For calendar year 2000, Tan Hup Thye received salary of $324,000, bonus of $480,000, and benefits in kind of $44,677, giving total income of $848,968, with bonus forming 56.5% of total income.
For calendar year 2001, Tan Hup Thye received salary of $324,000, bonus of $350,000, and benefits in kind of $8,373, giving total income of $682,373, with bonus forming 51.3% of total income.
For calendar year 2002, Tan Hup Thye received salary of $352,973, bonus of $218,674, and benefits in kind of $40,516, giving total income of $612,163, with bonus forming 35.6% of total income.
For calendar year 2003, Tan Hup Thye received salary of $324,000, bonus of $477,000, and benefits in kind of $95,561, giving total income of $896,561, with bonus forming 53.2% of total income.
For calendar year 2004, Tan Hup Thye received salary of $324,000, bonus of $922,164, and benefits in kind of $29,308, giving total income of $1,275,472, with bonus forming 72.3% of total income.
For calendar year 2005, Tan Hup Thye received salary of $299,455, bonus of $1,584,190, and benefits in kind of $30,239, giving total income of $1,913,884, with bonus forming 82.8% of total income.

From the financial year beginning 1 March 1996, Tan Hup Thye, as managing director, used a bonus formula based on 30% of pre-tax profits adjusted for cost of capital as a basis for determining the bonus pool for employees of Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd.

Between 1996 and 2005, at the end of each financial year, Tan Hup Thye prepared proposed bonus allocations for employees and sent bonus listings and summary sheets to Philip Roger Bennett for approval before payment.
By 26 October 2005 at the latest, Tan Hup Thye informed David Winterbottom of his intention to make bonus payments to the employees of Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd.

On 18 November 2005, Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd received a legal opinion from Rajah & Tann LLP regarding whether its employees were legally entitled to be paid bonuses prior to and as part of the orderly winding down or transfer of its business.

On 21 November 2005, a board meeting of Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd was held, attended in person by Tan Hup Thye and via teleconference by Keith Tay, at which a resolution referred to as the November resolution was passed approving payment of bonuses to employees totalling $6,485,094.
Later on 21 November 2005, Tan Hup Thye received an e-mail from Refco Inc's general counsel, Dennis Klejna, instructing him not to make any bonus payment without approval of the Refco Inc board.
On 22 November 2005, representatives from Refco Inc, including interim chief executive officer Robert Dangremond, told Tan Hup Thye not to make the bonus payments.

On 3 December 2005, the board of directors of Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd held a meeting referred to as the December meeting, attended by Tan Hup Thye and Keith Tay, at which a resolution referred to as the December Resolution was passed approving allocation of bonuses to individual employees according to a detailed bonus list, including a specified amount for Tan Hup Thye.

On 3 December 2005, minutes of the board meeting of Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd recorded section 6.0 titled 'Declaration of Bonus', noting review of a legal opinion from Rajah & Tann LLP and e-mail explanatory notes of 23 and 29 November 2005 to the interim chief executive officer of the ultimate parent, and resolving that bonuses be paid to employees as at 3 December 2005 based on profits earned from 1 March 2005 to 30 September 2005, with specific bonuses in an attached exhibit to be notified to future liquidators.

The bonus list associated with the December Resolution dated 3 December 2005 provided for a total sum of $6,485,097 to be distributed as bonuses among more than 180 employees, with $1,412,759 allocated to Tan Hup Thye.

On 4 December 2005, Laurence O'Connell from Man Financial (Singapore) Pte Ltd informed Tan Hup Thye that he should not communicate the November and December board resolutions on bonuses to the employees of Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd.

On 4 December 2005, Tan Hup Thye suggested to Laurence O'Connell that Man Financial (Singapore) Pte Ltd provide a specific assurance in its letters to employees that Man Financial Inc would honour applicable bonus entitlements, and O'Connell agreed to this suggestion.

On 25 November 2005, Rajah & Tann LLP sent a further letter to Tan Hup Thye stating that while it was arguable that employees were entitled to bonuses, the quantum might still be discretionary and that any discretion as to quantum should be exercised reasonably and in accordance with any agreed bonus criteria, and that if bonus payments were significantly higher than normal, the good faith of the board could be questioned.

On 1 April 2005, Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd implemented an Employee Policy and Practice Manual stating that employees would receive annual discretionary bonuses and/or profit sharing bonuses determined on the basis of the performance of the employee and his or her business unit, and requiring all employees, including Tan Hup Thye, to acknowledge and adhere to its contents.

On 20 April 2001, as managing director, Tan Hup Thye signed a letter of appointment to an employee, Ms Chan Hien Leng Dorothy, which stated that any bonus payment would depend on the company's performance and would be at the discretion of the company.

On 29 October 2003, as managing director, Tan Hup Thye signed a letter of appointment to an employee, Mr Chua Chiam Siang Jordan, which stated that he would be able to participate in any discretionary bonus arrangement that might be in force.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute conduct and documents, Tan Hup Thye had an enforceable right to receive bonus payments for the period 1 March 2005 to 4 December 2005 from Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd, including the amount specified for him in the 3 December 2005 board bonus allocation.
Party_Details.Rule: Under employment and company law, an employee's entitlement to bonus payments depends on the terms of the employment relationship and any applicable company policies or resolutions. Where bonus schemes are described as discretionary in contractual documents or policy manuals, the employer generally retains discretion whether to award and in what amount, unless there is a clear contractual term guaranteeing bonus or a clearly defined formula that is binding. Board resolutions allocating bonuses can create obligations if validly passed in accordance with the company's constitution and without disqualifying conflicts of interest or procedural defects.
Party_Details.Application: The pre-dispute materials Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd communicated a profit-sharing bonus framework to staff from 1996 onward, used a formula based on a percentage of pre-tax profits to provision for bonuses, and historically paid substantial bonuses to Tan Hup Thye as a large component of his remuneration. At the same time, letters of appointment signed by him for other employees and the Employee Policy and Practice Manual described bonuses as discretionary. For the 2005 financial year, bonus provisions were recorded in management accounts and a board meeting on 3 December 2005 approved a bonus allocation list that included a specific amount for him. Before that meeting, representatives of the ultimate shareholder instructed him not to make bonus payments, and the acquisition agreement with Man Financial contained restrictions on bonus commitments outside the ordinary course of business. These pre-dispute facts frame a conflict between the historical profit-sharing practice and documentation describing bonuses as discretionary, and between the internal December 2005 bonus allocation and the instructions and contractual constraints communicated by the shareholder and purchaser.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court analysed Article 83 of the defendant's Articles of Association and held that it prevented a director from voting on any matter arising out of a contract in which he was interested, including his own employment contract and bonus. It concluded that the plaintiff's vote on the December Resolution should not have been counted and that there was no proper quorum, rendering the resolution invalid. The court further reasoned that even if Article 83 did not apply, the December Resolution was not in the company's best interests because it exposed the company to breach of the Acquisition Agreement with Man Financial, which restricted bonus commitments outside the ordinary course of business, and because the shareholders, through Refco Inc, had expressly directed that no bonuses be paid. On the contractual entitlement issue, the court found that the plaintiff had no express contractual right to bonuses and that the company's practice of profit sharing via a 30% formula was not a binding guarantee but a guideline subject to shareholder approval. The court relied on evidence that bonus provisions in management accounts did not match final payouts, that bonuses were determined after the financial year end, that employees who left mid-year were not automatically paid bonuses, and that the plaintiff consistently sought shareholder approval for bonus payments. It applied Singapore authority, including Latham v Credit Suisse First Boston, to hold that where bonuses are described as discretionary, the employer's discretion is not constrained by the English line of cases requiring reasonableness in the exercise of bonus discretion. The court rejected the argument that communications by Man Financial referring to 'applicable bonus entitlements' amounted to recognition of a contractual right. On the counterclaim, the court held that the plaintiff breached fiduciary duties by procuring the December Resolution in the face of clear shareholder instructions and contractual constraints, and by relying on a legal opinion that had been given on incomplete facts. It found that the defendant became liable to pay bonuses to employees under settlement agreements as a result of the December Resolution and that the plaintiff must indemnify the defendant for losses, including amounts already paid and other expenses. The court therefore dismissed the plaintiff's claim for bonuses and entered judgment for the defendant on its counterclaim for damages to be assessed, with costs against the plaintiff.

Party_Details.Name: Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd (in members’ voluntary liquidation)

Facts.Text: Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd was incorporated in February 1984.
On 30 November 2006, Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd went into members' voluntary liquidation.
Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd was part of the Refco Group of companies, with its immediate holding company being Refco Global Holdings LLC and its ultimate shareholder being Refco Inc.
As of August 2005, the board of directors of Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd comprised Philip Roger Bennett, Tan Hup Thye, Santo Charles Maggio, and Keith Tay Ah Kee.
From April 1984, Philip Roger Bennett was the chairman and a director of Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
In 1999, Keith Tay Ah Kee was appointed as an independent director of Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
At all material times, Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd carried on business providing securities dealing services and broking services in financial futures contracts, foreign exchange, and commodities.
In August 2005, the Refco Group was restructured and Refco Inc was listed on the New York Stock Exchange.
In October 2005, Refco Inc announced that it had discovered a receivable of about US$430 million owing to it which had not been previously disclosed by Philip Roger Bennett.
On 17 October 2005, Refco Inc filed for and obtained bankruptcy protection in the United States.
Later in October 2005, Refco Inc retained AlixPartners LLC to assist with the potential sale of its businesses, including Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd.

AlixPartners LLC appointed KordaMentha, an Australian firm, to oversee the Refco Group's subsidiaries in Asia, including Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd, and KordaMentha appointed David Winterbottom, Cameron Duncan, and John Mouwad to oversee Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
On 16 November 2005, Robert Dangremond was appointed interim chief executive officer of Refco Inc.

On 13 November 2005, an Acquisition Agreement was entered into under which Man Financial Inc agreed to acquire the business of the Refco Group, including Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd, with various Refco Group entities, including Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd, as sellers.

Under the 13 November 2005 Acquisition Agreement, it was agreed that the assets and business operations of Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd would be taken over by Man Financial (Singapore) Pte Ltd on 5 December 2005.

Section 8.1(d) of the Acquisition Agreement dated 13 November 2005 provided that each party would not make commitments to pay on or after the closing date any bonuses or other benefits to employees of the business other than in the ordinary course of business.

Sections 8.17(a) and (b) of the Acquisition Agreement dated 13 November 2005 required Man Financial to make a general offer of employment to employees of Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd on terms of remuneration and other benefits comparable to those they enjoyed prior to closing.

On 22 November 2005, Tan Hup Thye wrote to Robert Dangremond seeking consent and approval from Refco Inc, as manager of Refco Global Holdings LLC, to make bonus payments to employees of Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd for the period 1 March 2005 to 30 September 2005.

On 23 November 2005, Robert Dangremond instructed Tan Hup Thye that he was not to make any payment of bonus and stated that there was no room to discuss the issue as the Refco Inc board would not approve the payment.

On 28 November 2005, Man Financial (Singapore) Pte Ltd sent an e-mail to all employees of Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd stating that Man Financial wanted to put their minds at rest over the issue of bonuses and assuring them that it would honour all applicable bonus entitlements in the normal course at the relevant time of year.

On 8 December 2005, Daniel Yeo, managing director of Man Financial, informed former employees of Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd that any applicable bonus entitlements that would have been due to them at the relevant time after Refco's year end if they had remained employees of Refco would be computed using the same methodology as used by Refco in previous years and payable in April 2006.
On 5 December 2005, pursuant to the Acquisition Agreement, a transfer agreement was entered into and the employees of Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd were transferred to Man Financial (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
On 5 December 2005, Man Financial (Singapore) Pte Ltd took over the business of Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd and integrated it into its own operations.

In April 2006, Man Financial (Singapore) Pte Ltd paid bonuses to former employees of Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd who remained employed with Man Financial (Singapore) Pte Ltd, using amounts corresponding to bonus summary sheets and listings prepared while they were employed by Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd.

By 30 November 2006, Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd had been placed in members' voluntary winding up, and liquidators were appointed to follow up on matters including any bonus notifications arising from the December 2005 board minutes.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute conduct, documents, and instructions, Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd was obliged to pay the bonus amounts specified for Tan Hup Thye in the 3 December 2005 bonus allocation and whether it could recover from him losses arising from bonus-related settlement payments to former employees.
Party_Details.Rule: A company is bound to pay bonuses to an employee only if there is a valid contractual or other legal obligation, such as a binding employment term or a validly passed board resolution within the company's constitutional and contractual limits. Where bonus schemes are described as discretionary in employment documents or policy manuals, the company generally retains discretion whether to award bonuses and in what amounts, unless that discretion is clearly limited. A company may seek recovery from an officer if that officer's actions, taken in the course of managing the company, cause the company to incur liabilities through decisions that are inconsistent with the company's constitutional provisions, shareholder instructions, or external contractual obligations.
Party_Details.Application: Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd operated a profit-sharing bonus framework and historically provisioned and paid bonuses, but its written employment documents and the Employee Policy and Practice Manual described bonuses as discretionary. In November 2005, its ultimate shareholder, through Refco Inc and its interim chief executive officer, instructed that no bonuses were to be paid. The Acquisition Agreement with Man Financial restricted bonus commitments outside the ordinary course of business. On 3 December 2005, the board of Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd passed a resolution allocating bonuses, including a large amount to Tan Hup Thye, and providing for notification of these amounts to future liquidators. This internal allocation occurred after the shareholder's instructions and in the context of the Acquisition Agreement's restrictions. Subsequently, Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd faced bonus claims from former employees and made settlement payments. These pre-dispute facts set up a question whether the company had any binding obligation to pay the specific bonus amounts to Tan Hup Thye and whether the internal December 2005 allocation, made in the face of prior instructions and contractual constraints, could be a basis for shifting the financial impact of settlement payments back to him.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court held that Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd was not contractually obliged to pay the plaintiff the bonuses he claimed. It found that the company's bonus practice, including the 30% profit-based formula, did not amount to a binding contractual guarantee and that the Employee Policy and Practice Manual and appointment letters clearly characterised bonuses as discretionary. The court concluded that the December Resolution was invalid because the plaintiff's interest in his own bonus meant he could not vote under Article 83, leaving the meeting without a proper quorum. It further held that, even if valid, the resolution was not in the company's best interests because it conflicted with the Acquisition Agreement and express shareholder instructions not to pay bonuses. On the counterclaim, the court determined that the plaintiff breached fiduciary duties by procuring the December Resolution in these circumstances and by relying on a legal opinion that had been given without full disclosure of relevant facts. As a result, the company incurred liabilities to former employees and associated expenses. The court therefore dismissed the plaintiff's claim and entered judgment for Refco (Singapore) Pte Ltd on its counterclaim, ordering that damages be assessed and that the plaintiff bear the company's costs.
